what_it_does: Declares a source-anchored design-language decision record (a single DECISION dictionary) that documents an unresolved identity fork over which geometric shape — octagon or hexagon — represents the company's single most central diagram mark, with options, implications, scope, provenance, and legibility metadata.
observations: The file is a data-only Python module containing one DECISION dictionary and no executable code symbols.
It records a trade-off between two mutually exclusive shape-language options: octagon-as-core versus hexagon-as-core.
It explicitly states there is no recommended option and the owner (Tim) must make the call.
Provenance is anchored to a specific JSON file path and key in the source design review output.
It embeds a follow-up note that card_variant render treatment may need a shape preview, but marks it as not an owner question.
The shape hierarchy rule is that rarer shapes carry more important meaning, with octagon rarest and hexagon next.
standouts: It is an unresolved identity fork presented as a formal decision record rather than a resolved rule.
It links the decision to a verbatim-verified source JSON provenance path.
It encodes a visual-semantics rule: shape rarity maps to importance.
conventions: Follows a structured decision-record schema with id, meaning, options, scope, subtype, explanation_source, and legibility.
Uses a module-level docstring to explain provenance, context, and follow-up notes.
Breaks typical Python module conventions by containing only a data declaration with no functions, classes, or imports.
concerns: The decision is still open, so any downstream diagram or render relying on it is blocked until the owner resolves it.
A render refinement note is embedded in a comment rather than tracked as a separate actionable item.
The provenance path is absolute and may become brittle if the repository or extraction layout changes.
notes: This file is a design-decision ledger entry that captures the unresolved 'which shape is the core' identity fork for Tim, preserving both options and their provenance but offering no recommendation.
questions: Which specific source decks or diagrams were compared to surface this inconsistency?
What criteria or process will be used to choose between the octagon and hexagon options?
How will the chosen core shape be rendered in card_variant treatments?
Who besides Tim is authorized to resolve or update this decision?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file documents the core-shape identity fork exactly as its docstring and DECISION structure describe.
intention_signals: It is trying to surface and preserve a genuine ambiguity in the company's visual language, present the two possible shape hierarchies with their implications, and hand the unresolved choice to the owner with full context and a source-anchored audit trail.
novelty: It treats a visual-design identity question — which geometric shape is central — as a versioned, schema-structured, source-anchored decision record rather than an informal comment or slide note.
contribution: It contributes a structured, source-anchored record of a design-language identity fork, preserving both candidate shape hierarchies and their implications so that downstream diagrams, decks, and render treatments can align consistently once the owner resolves the choice.
summary_for_embedding: A data-only Python decision record that documents an unresolved company design-language fork over whether the octagon or the hexagon represents the single most central shape in diagrams, including options, implications, provenance, and legibility metadata.
intention_for_embedding: To surface and preserve a source-verified identity decision about the company's core diagram shape so the owner can resolve it consistently across decks and renders.
